angle.headline: Why Your M&A Diligence Process Finds Everything Except What Kills Deals
angle.thesis: Middle-market owners spend six figures on diligence that audits the past but never diagnoses whether the target can operate without its founder — the single variable that determines post-close performance.
angle.targetPersona: Business owners in growth-through-acquisition mode, particularly those who've completed one deal and are now considering their second, operating with $10M-$75M revenue across the US-LATAM corridor
angle.relevanceToAudience: With USMCA at its six-year review and nearshoring momentum creating acquisition opportunities across the corridor, owners are evaluating targets in Mexico and LATAM — but standard diligence frameworks were built for US-only deals and miss the founder-dependency risk that is magnified across borders and jurisdictions.
talkingPoints: Most M&A diligence answers 'Does this business make money?' but never asks 'Can this business make decisions without the seller in the room?' — the real post-close question.

The failure happens because diligence is structured to audit the past (financials, compliance, IP) rather than diagnose the operating model's ability to execute independently — particularly across time zones and legal systems in corridor deals.

The Machine Test: before you sign the LOI, spend one week asking only this: 'Who makes what decisions when the owner is unavailable?' If the answer is 'it waits' or 'it escalates,' you are buying a job, not a business.
Add one diligence workstream focused exclusively on decision rights, escalation patterns, and information flow — not as compliance check, but as operating model architecture assessment.

peopleAngle.challenge: The seller's leadership team has never operated without the founder's real-time input, and the buyer's integration team lacks the trust or context to make decisions during the 90-day transition window when founder dependency becomes operationally visible.
peopleAngle.framework: Kotter Step 2: Build the Guiding Coalition

entries.fieldReportSummary: [Meta announced this week](https://www.businessinsider.com/meta-layoffs-internal-memo-ai-job-cuts-faq-2026-5) that it will cut 10% of staff on Wednesday, eliminating around 8,000 roles in three waves of notifications timed to 4am local time across regions. angle.headline: When Your Best People Leave Because You Won't Let Go
angle.thesis: Business owners who refuse to delegate strategic decisions aren't protecting the business — they're guaranteeing that the people capable of scaling it will leave for environments where their judgment matters.
angle.targetPersona: $15M–$75M business owners who have built the company through personal relationships and instinct, now watching high-potential managers plateau or exit despite offering competitive compensation
angle.relevanceToAudience: The US-LATAM corridor creates unique succession pressure: institutional buyers expect scalable operations, but owner-operators who built through personal networks treat delegation as loss of control rather than as the architecture of sale-readiness.
talkingPoints: The pattern: you hire talented operators, watch them succeed in execution, then override their strategic recommendations because 'they don't see the full picture.'
The diagnosis: avoiding critical conversations about decision rights creates a revolving door where capable people leave not for more money but for more authority.
The framework: decision rights mapping — explicitly defining which decisions require owner approval, which require consultation, and which belong fully to functional leaders.

The action: before your next strategic meeting, write down the three decisions you will delegate completely this quarter and announce them with the constraint that you cannot reverse them for 90 days.

peopleAngle.challenge: The owner must shift from being the person with the best answers to being the person who builds the system that produces good answers — requiring them to trust others' judgment on decisions where their own instinct has historically been correct.

entries.fieldReportSummary: LongRange Capital agreed this week to acquire Pizza Hut from Yum Brands for $1.5 billion, with Yum China Holdings taking the Mainland China business for an additional $1.2 billion, according to [Middle Market Growth](https://middlemarketgrowth.org/pe-weekly-pizza-hut-take-private-tech-deals/).
